title: MAPWaters Act of 2025
plain_summary: The MAPWaters Act of 2025 requires the Forest Service and Department of the Interior to create online databases that show the public where they can use federal waterways for boating, fishing, and other water activities. These agencies must work together to make sure their computer systems can share information easily and that all the data follows the same standards. Within five years, they must put maps and information online showing which waterways people can access, what restrictions exist, and where fishing is allowed or not allowed. The agencies must update boating and waterway information at least twice a year and update fishing rules right away when they change. The public will also be able to ask questions or make comments about the information provided.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Modernizing Access to our Public Waters Act of two thousand and twenty-five or the MAPWaters Act of two thousand and twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms mean the following. A Federal fishing restriction is an area where the government temporarily or permanently stops or limits all fishing or certain types of fishing. A Federal land or water management agency means the Bureau of Reclamation, the National Park Service, the Bureau of Land Management, the United States Fish and Wildlife Service, and the Forest Service. A Federal waterway means waters that are managed by one or more of the government officials called Secretaries. A Federal waterway restriction means a rule that limits how people can access or use a Federal waterway. The rule is made by one or more of the Secretaries under existing laws. Secretaries means the Secretary of Agriculture who works through the Chief of the Forest Service and the Secretary of the Interior. State means each of the fifty states, the District of Columbia, and each territory of the United States.

sections.title: Section 3 Interagency Data Standardization
sections.text: No later than thirty months after this law is passed, the Secretaries must work together with the Federal Geographic Data Committee to create shared standards. These standards will make sure that different government databases can work together when they collect and share location information about public outdoor recreation access to Federal waterways and Federal fishing restrictions.

sections.title: Section 4 Data Consolidation and Publication
sections.text: No later than five years after this law is passed, each Secretary must digitize and put online geographic information system data about Federal waterway restrictions. This includes information about when Federal waterways are open or closed to boats and people, including requirements for boat inspections and cleaning, low-flying aircraft, or diving. It includes dates when waterways are seasonally closed. It includes areas where motorized boats, certain horsepower levels, or fuel types are restricted. It includes areas with anchoring restrictions, no wake zones, exclusion zones, danger areas, or boat speed limits. It includes restrictions on which direction boats can travel, such as upstream or downstream. It includes what activities are allowed in each area, such as canoes and paddlecraft, rafts and driftboats, motorboats, personal watercraft, airboats, amphibious aircraft, hovercraft, snowmobiles and other motorized vehicles on frozen water, oceangoing ships, swimming, and other recreational activities as determined by the Secretaries. Each Secretary must also digitize and put online information about Federal waterway access and navigation. This includes the location of boat ramps, portages, and fishing access sites that the Federal agencies control. It includes when these facilities and sites are open or closed. It includes underwater depth information and depth charts when possible. Each Secretary must also digitize and put online information about Federal fishing restrictions. This includes the location and boundaries of areas where recreational and commercial fishing is restricted, including full or partial closures, no-take zones, and fishing restrictions in or around marine protected areas. It includes restrictions on using specific types of equipment or bait. It includes Federal requirements about catch and release fishing. The Secretaries must create a way for the public to ask questions or make comments about this information. The Secretaries must update the waterway restriction and access information at least two times per year. They must update the fishing restriction information in real time as changes happen. This section does not apply to irrigation canals and flowage easements (legal agreements that allow water to flow across property). Any geographic information made public under this section cannot reveal details about the nature, location, character, or ownership of historic, paleontological (fossil-related), or archaeological resources, following existing laws.

sections.title: Section 5 Cooperation and Coordination
sections.text: To carry out this law, the Secretaries can work with and partner with non-Federal agencies and private sector and nonprofit partners. These include State natural resource agencies, Tribal natural resource agencies, technology companies, geospatial data companies, and experts in data science, analytics, and operations research. The Secretaries can make agreements with third parties to carry out any part of this law. The Secretaries can work with the Director of the United States Geological Survey to collect, combine, digitize, standardize, and publish data on behalf of the Secretaries to meet the requirements of this law. The Secretaries must develop the data following all applicable Federal, State, and Tribal laws and regulations. They must include a notice that any location data is subject to applicable Federal, State, and Tribal laws and regulations. When possible, the Secretary must use or include existing applicable data, maps, and resources when carrying out this law. This includes data, maps, and resources developed under the Modernizing Access to Our Public Land Act, section one hundred and three of division DD of the Consolidated Appropriations Act of two thousand and twenty-three, or other applicable laws.

sections.title: Section 6 Reports
sections.text: No later than one year after this law is passed and then every year until March thirtieth, two thousand and thirty-four, the Secretaries must submit a report describing their progress in meeting the requirements of this law. They must send this report to the Committee on Natural Resources of the House of Representatives, the Committee on Energy and Commerce of the House of Representatives, the Committee on Agriculture of the House of Representatives, the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources of the Senate, and the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry of the Senate.

sections.title: Section 7 Effect
sections.text: Nothing in this law changes or alters the definition of navigable waters under Federal law. This law does not affect the jurisdiction or authority of State or Federal agencies to regulate navigable waters. This law does not change or alter the authority or jurisdiction of Federal or State agencies to manage fisheries. This law does not authorize or intend to result in a change in the accessibility of waters open to hunting, fishing, or other forms of outdoor recreation as they existed on the date this law was passed.
